bio.notes: Middle name honors maternal grandfather's family
bio.title: Colonel
bio.photo_notes: Portrait of Col. Buckingham St. John Lockwood

burial.plot_notes: FindAGrave — East Norwalk Historical Cemetery. Died Feb 10, 1850 aged 75.

narrative_blocks.header: St. John and Lockwood families intermarried each generation
narrative_blocks.body: The Lockwood family historian documented that St. Johns and Lockwoods had intermarried in nearly every generation since the founding of Norwalk. Buckingham's own mother was Susannah St. John, making his 1808 marriage to Polly Esther St. John the continuation of a dynastic alliance running across at least four consecutive generations.

narrative_blocks.header: Last witness to where Norwalk's first church stood
narrative_blocks.body: By 1847, at seventy-three, Buckingham was quoted by Norwalk's city historian on the exact location of the town's original meeting-house — a site he could still picture as open common ground. He was among the last living residents with a direct memory of colonial Norwalk's original layout.

bio.photo_notes: Portrait of Mary Esther (St. John) Lockwood

cross_connections.display_label: Born St. John; wed Lockwood second cousin in 1808

narrative_blocks.header: Born St. John; wed a Lockwood second cousin
narrative_blocks.body: Polly Esther St. John married Buckingham St. John Lockwood in 1808 — her second cousin through their common great-grandmother Susannah Selleck St. John of Norwalk, continuing the St. John-Lockwood intermarriage pattern across yet another generation. Her father William Saint John and his grandmother Susannah Saint John Lockwood were siblings.

parents.father_research_notes: Grandson of Rev. Edward Taylor of Westfield MA, the Puritan poet (1642-1729).

burial.plot_notes: FindAGrave #47532082 (wife Mary's listing cross-references John). Died Apr 8, 1876 aged 64.

notable.notable_blurb: Co-owner St. Nicholas Hotel NYC; New Milford builder
notable.primary_url_label: Wikipedia — St. Nicholas Hotel (New York City)

career.organization: St. Nicholas Hotel, New York City
career.notes: 600-room luxury hotel opening Jan 6, 1853; expanded to 1,000 rooms by end of year. First NYC building to cost over $1 million.

career.organization: Homestead Inn, 5 Elm Street, New Milford CT
career.notes: Built as private residence 1853; housed three generations of Treadwells until 1915. Now operates as a bed and breakfast.

narrative_blocks.header: Built New York's first million-dollar hotel
narrative_blocks.body: Treadwell co-owned the St. Nicholas Hotel when it opened on January 6, 1853 — a 600-room luxury hotel that was the first New York City building to cost over $1 million. By year's end it had expanded to 1,000 rooms, setting a new standard for lavish appointments in American hospitality.

narrative_blocks.header: Hotel ended the Astor House's fifty-year reign
narrative_blocks.body: When the St. Nicholas opened, it directly challenged the Astor House — Manhattan's preeminent hotel since 1836 — and within months had displaced it as the city's premier destination. Treadwell had helped engineer a decisive shift in Manhattan's hotel hierarchy.

narrative_blocks.header: Built New Milford estate same year hotel opened
narrative_blocks.body: In 1853, the same year the St. Nicholas launched in New York, Treadwell built the Homestead Inn at 5 Elm Street in New Milford as his private residence. The house sheltered three generations of Treadwells until 1915 and still operates today as a bed and breakfast.

sources.label: Wikipedia — St. Nicholas Hotel (New York City)

burial.plot_notes: FindAGrave. Center Cemetery New Milford CT.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at three
narrative_blocks.body: Julia Abigail Lockwood Treadwell died in August 1849 at age three, and her parents gave the same name to their next daughter born in December 1852. The second Julia Abigail also died young, at thirty-two, in November 1884.

burial.plot_notes: FindAGrave. Center Cemetery New Milford CT.

narrative_blocks.header: Named for dead sister
narrative_blocks.body: Her parents had lost their first daughter Julia Abigail to childhood death in 1849 and gave the same name to this daughter when she was born in 1852. She married — taking the name McRoberts — but like her namesake, died at thirty-two in November 1884.

narrative_blocks.header: William died young
narrative_blocks.body: William died young; the year was not recorded. His parents gave the name to a brother born two years later.

bio.photo_notes: Portrait of Frederick St. John Lockwood (Yale 1849)

notable.notable_blurb: Yale 1849; Norwalk banker, railroad president, legislator

education.notes: A.B. 1849. Classmate of Timothy Dwight (future 8th Yale president). Captain of the Augusta boat race crew — credited as founding vessel of the Yale Navy.

career.organization: Union Army — staffs of Maj.-Gen. King and Maj.-Gen. Russell
career.notes: Civil War service details not fully documented.

military_service.unit: Staffs of Major-Generals King and Russell
military_service.notes: Specific unit and rank not documented in Lockwood genealogy or Patch.com biography.

institutions.institution_blurb: Yale A.B. 1849; classmate of Timothy Dwight, future 8th Yale president.

cross_connections.display_label: Yale Class of 1849; Dwight became the eighth university president

narrative_blocks.header: Captained Yale Navy's founding boat, 1845
narrative_blocks.body: In August 1845, Lockwood captained the Augusta, an eight-oared cedar shell his classmates bought for $170, later identified as the vessel that launched what became the Yale Navy. He graduated with the class of 1849 alongside Timothy Dwight, who would become the university's eighth president.

narrative_blocks.header: Staffed two Union generals; built Norwalk's banking empire
narrative_blocks.body: After serving on the staffs of Major-Generals King and Russell in the Civil War, Lockwood became president of Fairfield County National Bank and the Danbury and Norwalk Railroad, and served three terms as a Republican representative in the Connecticut General Assembly.

narrative_blocks.header: SAR member; great-grandfather burned out by British
narrative_blocks.body: A century after British forces torched Norwalk and destroyed his great-grandfather's home, Lockwood joined the Connecticut Society of the Sons of the American Revolutionary War, a membership whose meaning ran close to home. He was also made a Knight Templar and remained active in the Norwalk Club until his final years.

burial.plot_notes: FindAGrave #73358435. Hubbard Memorial Cemetery, Huron City MI.

institutions.institution_blurb: Vassar College 1901; multi-sport champion at tennis, golf, and sailing.

narrative_blocks.header: Vassar champion in tennis, golf, sailing, and horses
narrative_blocks.body: After graduating Vassar in 1901, Lockwood became an accomplished sportswoman who took prizes in tennis, golf, and sailing tournaments and rode with the Oxbridge Hunt Club in Darien. A world traveler many times over, she made her last circumnavigation of the globe in 1938.

narrative_blocks.header: Rockies horseback trip led to California farmhouse
narrative_blocks.body: In the early 1920s, Lockwood and college friend Mayone Lewis rode on horseback through the Canadian Rockies and decided on the trip to make California their future home. They built a Norwegian farmhouse there in 1925, and Lockwood lived in California until her death in Pasadena in 1976 at ninety-four.

narrative_blocks.header: Ran successful peach farm after agricultural college
narrative_blocks.body: Lockwood and Lewis attended both Storrs and Amherst Agricultural Colleges and then co-owned Blythefield Farm in Norwalk, running a successful commercial peach operation — an unusual choice for a Vassar-educated sportswoman of her era. She returned east regularly for Vassar reunions throughout her long California years.

parents.father_research_notes: Per Lockwood genealogy + EH. Wikipedia says mother was 'Mary Hawley' — discrepancy with Lockwood genealogy 'Abigail (Hawley) Lockwood.' Using genealogy/EH as higher-priority source.

notable.notable_blurb: Nine-term Connecticut legislator; Revolutionary War captain

military_service.unit: First Company, Colonel Charles Webb's Seventh Connecticut Regiment
military_service.notes: Enlisted Jul 12, 1775; discharged Dec 24, 1775. Assigned to Gen. Sullivan's Brigade on Winter Hill. Assistant Commissary of Issues 1778; captain of coast guards 1780.

career.notes: Posted $5,000 bond to Henry Laurens, President of the Continental Congress, Jul 21, 1778.
Represented Norwalk nine sessions: May/Oct 1790, May/Oct 1791, Oct 1794, May/Oct 1795, May/Oct 1796.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost home when British torched Norwalk, 1779
narrative_blocks.body: British forces burned Norwalk in July 1779, destroying Lockwood's home among hundreds of others along the shore. He had already served in the 7th Connecticut Regiment and organized a petition to the General Assembly for an armed sloop after British ships raided the harbor at night.

narrative_blocks.header: Organized Norwalk's first volunteer fire department
narrative_blocks.body: After the war Lockwood organized the first voluntary fire department in Norwalk, building civic infrastructure in the town the British had burned. He went on to represent Norwalk nine terms in the Connecticut General Assembly between 1790 and 1796.

narrative_blocks.header: Petitioned Assembly for armed sloop; British still came
narrative_blocks.body: In October 1777, Lockwood joined neighbors in petitioning the Connecticut General Assembly for an armed sloop to patrol Long Island Sound after British vessels repeatedly raided Norwalk's harbor at night, firing on houses and stealing livestock. Two years later, the raids he had warned against culminated in the burning of the town that destroyed his home.

parents.father_name: Capt. Joseph St. John of Norwalk
parents.father_research_notes: Capt. Joseph St. John of Norwalk CT; d. 1756. His estate included enslaved people.

narrative_blocks.header: Orphaned at six; married the town's future captain
narrative_blocks.body: Her mother Susannah Selleck died in December 1749 when Susannah was barely six, and her father Capt. Joseph St. John died in 1756 when she was thirteen, leaving her to grow up in Norwalk without either parent. She married Captain Eliphalet Lockwood at thirty-three — he would go on to lose his home to British torches and organize the city's first volunteer fire department.

narrative_blocks.header: Earned 'Blue Sheep' nickname by dyeing his flock
narrative_blocks.body: Tradition holds that Capt. Joseph Saint John attempted to dye a sheep blue to create a matching flock — and the rains washed it out, leaving him with the lasting nickname 'The Blue Sheep.' He was a large landowner in Norwalk and among its founding civic leaders, serving as Selectman, Rate Constable, Fence Viewer, and Captain of the 1st Company of the Norwalk trainband (1748).

narrative_blocks.header: Wife Susanna's epitaph: Rachel's face, Leah's fruitful womb
narrative_blocks.body: When Susanna Selleck Saint John died in December 1749 at forty, Capt. Joseph commissioned a tombstone epitaph comparing her to Rachel ('face'), Leah ('fruitful womb'), Abigail ('wisdom'), Lydia ('faithful heart'), and Martha ('care'). The epitaph — one of the most elaborate in the East Norwalk churchyard — captures how colonial Norwalk elites memorialized women through accumulated biblical virtues.

burial.plot_notes: East Norwalk Historical Cemetery, Norwalk, Fairfield, Connecticut

H00253.gravestone_inscription: Susanna St. John, ye wife of Capt. Joseph St. John died December ye 4th, 1749 aged 40 years and 2 months. "She that lies at rest within this tomb / Had Rachel's face and Leah's fruitful womb, / Abigail's wisdom, Lydia's faithful heart / With Martha's care — we hope Mary's better part."

enslaved_persons.name: Jenny
enslaved_persons.description: Enslaved woman. Listed in estate inventory of Joseph St. John, Dec 7, 1756.

enslaved_persons.name: London
enslaved_persons.description: Enslaved man. Listed in estate inventory of Joseph St. John, Dec 7, 1756.

enslaved_persons.name: Peter
enslaved_persons.description: Enslaved boy. Listed in estate inventory of Joseph St. John, Dec 7, 1756.

narrative_blocks.header: Her mother died one month after her birth
narrative_blocks.body: She was born September 3, 1709 in Norwalk. Her mother Hannah Hooker died October 5, 1709 — barely one month later, age nineteen, of complications from childbirth.

narrative_blocks.header: Married 'The Blue Sheep' captain in 1729
narrative_blocks.body: She married Capt. Joseph St. John in 1729 — known as 'The Blue Sheep' for the local tradition that he once dyed a sheep blue, though the rains washed it out. He served as Norwalk selectman seven times across thirty years.

narrative_blocks.header: Three generations of slaveholding descendants
narrative_blocks.body: After her death, her husband's 1756 estate inventory listed three enslaved people: Jenny, London, and Peter. Three consecutive generations of her descendants held enslaved people — including grandson William St. John, who sent his enslaved man Dover to the Continental Army in his own place.

burialCemetery.name: East Norwalk Historical Cemetery
burialCemetery.access_notes: Sometimes locked; managed by local taxing district
features: First Settlers of Norwalk Memorial (lists 31 individuals from 1649)
Grave of Thomas 'Yankee Doodle' Fitch IV (1724-1795)
burialCemetery.notes: Oldest cemetery in Norwalk; triangle-shaped; near East Norwalk railway station. Mary Willet Hooker Buckingham buried here, near center of burying ground near East Norwalk railway station.
